coredata.dc:title: Achieving high-performance flexible UV photodetectors: modulating oxygen vacancies via hydroxymethylated PEDOT and surface functionalities of N-doped graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a flexible UV photodetector with an organic-inorganic hybrid structure, fabricated using advanced face-to-face self-assembly. The detector is composed of PEDOT-CH2OH and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) modified zinc oxide nanoflowers (ZnO NFs). The sulfur and hydroxyl groups in PEDOT-CH2OH, along with nitrogen and oxygen functional groups in N-GQDs, promote the formation and stabilization of oxygen vacancies (Vo) on the ZnO NFs surface. This significantly enhances carrier separation efficiency, reduces electron-hole recombination, and improves the detector's performance. Under 365 nm UV light, the device achieves a response rate of 1.31 mA W−1 at 0V bias, with fast rise times (0.078 s) and fall time (0.093 s). The detector shows excellent stability and rapid photonic response, providing solid support for wearable electronics and new theoretical guidance for ZnO photodetector improvements.

head.ce:title: Achieving high-performance flexible UV photodetectors: modulating oxygen vacancies via hydroxymethylated PEDOT and surface functionalities of N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a flexible UV photodetector with an organic-inorganic hybrid structure, fabricated using advanced face-to-face self-assembly. The detector is composed of PEDOT-CH2OH and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) modified zinc oxide nanoflowers (ZnO NFs). The sulfur and hydroxyl groups in PEDOT-CH2OH, along with nitrogen and oxygen functional groups in N-GQDs, promote the formation and stabilization of oxygen vacancies (Vo) on the ZnO NFs surface. This significantly enhances carrier separation efficiency, reduces electron-hole recombination, and improves the detector's performance. Under 365 nm UV light, the device achieves a response rate of 1.31 mA W−1 at 0V bias, with fast rise times (0.078 s) and fall time (0.093 s). The detector shows excellent stability and rapid photonic response, providing solid support for wearable electronics and new theoretical guidance for ZnO photodetector improvements.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) photodetector as a photoelectric device that can utilize the photoelectric effect of semiconductors to achieve photoelectric conversion, have received more and more attention for their applications in ultraviolet astronomy, ultraviolet imaging, etc [1]. The traditional rigid UV PDs, due to the limitation of brittle substrate materials, are susceptible to fracture or even breakage under a certain degree of bending or external mechanical force, which makes them difficult to be applied to harsh operating conditions such as bending [2]. In order to pursue the quality of lightweight, thin substrate, impact resistance, flexible strength is not easy to break, so that it can withstand bending and other harsh operating conditions, flexible wearable sensors came into being, and its portable and flexible technology is a new goal that we are constantly pursuing.

Photodetectors have been designed in various structures, including p-n homojunctions or heterojunctions, and metal-semiconductor-metal (MSM) configurations [3]. Among these, metal oxide (MOS)-based heterojunction UV photodetectors are gaining importance due to their ability to form a built-in electric field for self-powered detection. For example, Ozel [4,5] et al. used metal oxides like TiO2 and SnO2, which have wide bandgaps, to absorb UV light. By forming heterojunctions with silicon, they modified the segregation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, thereby enhancing the photodetector's performance. In addition, doping metal oxides can further boost carrier concentration and mobility, improving heterojunction quality [6,7].

ZnO, a third-generation wide-bandgap semiconductor, stands out due to its broadly gap, good optical transparency, and high exciton binding energy. It's easy and inexpensive preparation method makes it widely adopted in optoelectronics [3]. Importantly, ZnO can be combined with flexible substrates such as polyimide, making it suitable for flexible wearable devices [8]. Recent years have seen significant progress in ZnO-based UV photodetectors. For instance, Jalal [9] et al. reported that tungsten-doped ZnO thin films improved light absorption and carrier mobility. Mitra [10] et al. developed a deep UV PD using quantum dots combined with ZnO, showing excellent photoresponse in the 200–300 nm range. Li [11] et al. proposed a visible-blind UV PD based on single-walled carbon nanotube films and ZnO heterostructures, highlighting carbon nanotubes' potential to improve ZnO device behavior.

The construction of flexible metal-oxide-based heterojunction UV detectors relies on UV light irradiation of metal oxides, where photon energy absorption causes valence band electrons to jump to the conduction band, shaping photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These pairs migrate under internal or external electric fields to create detectable current signals [12]. Common ZnO-based UV photodetectors come in forms like nanoparticles, nanowires, nanotubes, and nanorods. However, ZnO's inherent rigidity makes nanorods prone to cracks or defects during bending, affecting device stability and longevity [13–15]. Traditional ZnO photodetectors typically depend on external voltages to drive photogenerated carrier separation and migration, leading to energy loss and potential heat generation [16]. In contrast, forming p-n heterojunctions with inorganic metal oxide semiconductors and organic semiconductors creates a built-in electric field. This facilitates photogenerated carrier separation and migration [17]. This design not only boosts carrier separation efficiency and self-powered performance but also leverages the flexibility of petal-like ZnO sheets. The structure remains stable under bending, suiting it for portable, flexible, self-powered UV detectors. Conducting polymers like PPy, PTh, PEDOT, and PANI are often used as p-type semiconductors due to their excellent hole-transporting abilities [18,19]. Among them, sulfur-containing PEDOT derivatives, such as PEDOT-CH2OH, are notable. Its flexible chains and rigid platelets allow the polymer to deform, enhancing its flexibility.

However, in practical applications, ZnO's high rate of photogenerated carrier recombination significantly reduces light absorption efficiency and causes fluctuations in optoelectronic properties [20]. These issues are closely tied to ZnO's surface and interfacial structure, particularly the Vo on its surface, which are a key factor in influencing its optoelectronic performance [21–25]. As a common defect in ZnO, Vo can greatly alter the material's electronic structure and optical properties by acting as electron trapping or release centers, thereby affecting the course of photogenerated carrier formation, separation, and conveyance [26]. In UV detectors, the concentration and distribution of Vo directly determine the lifetime, recombination rate, and migration behavior of photogenerated carriers. For instance, regulating the concentration of Vo through doping or the synthesis of nanostructures can enhance UV absorption and the photoelectric conversion efficiency [27–29]. QDs connected with zinc oxide nanomaterials can improve photoconductivity and reduce carrier recombination in photodetectors, among GQDs have gained attention due to high fluorescence activity, and excellent photostability, which can also be used as photosensitizers to enhance ZnO's light absorption ability. Importantly, GQDs can strongly interact with oxygen vacancies to stabilize them, thereby promoting electron-hole separation efficiency [30–32]. For example, Chen [33] et al. demonstrated that oxygen vacancies and graphene materials can significantly improve electron-hole separation efficiency when combined. Xiong [34]et al. designed a Bi2WO6 catalyst co-modified by surface Vo and GQDs, showing strong visible to near-infrared region absorption, which highlights the strong interaction between GQDs and metal oxide surface Vo. Therefore, studying the effect of Vo on the behavior of photogenerated carriers on ZnO surfaces and their regular impact on UV PD performance is a valid tactics for enhancing the photoelectric performance of UV PDs.

In this work, a flexible UV PD with a structure based on ZnO NFs: N-CQDs/PEDOT-CH2OH was prepared by face-to-face self-assembly method. The detector possesses excellent photoresponsiveness and short photoresponse time. The self-powered function is achieved by constructing a gap-breaking organic-inorganic heterojunction, and the lecels of Vo on the ZnO is optimized by N-GQDs, which, in combination with its effect on the roughness of the ZnO surface, dramatically improves the process of carrier create, segregation, and propagation, and then significantly enhances the capability of the UV PD. The results show that the detector exhibits a top responsivity of 1.31 mA W−1, a top detectivity of 4.03 × 1010 Jones, and a best rise/fall time of 0.078 s/0.093 s. In addition, the detector maintained good detection activity after 150° bending and 1000 bending cycles. This study inspects the effect of Vo on the formation, movement and interaction of photogenerated carriers on the ZnO surface. It offers novel theoretical perspectives for reinforce the efficiency of ZnO-based UV PDs by modulating the Vo.

Hydroxymethyl 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT-CH2OH), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (ZnNO3·6H2O), tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6), methylene chloride (CH2Cl2), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), and all of the above are ready to be used, and the reagents are from Aladdin. Conductive film ITO/PET from Shenzhen Rigorous Technology Co.,Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PEDOT-CH2OH on ITO substrates
ce:section.ce:para: PEDOT-CH2OH was prepared by electrochemical polymerization on ITO/PET substrate (see Fig. S1 for electrochemical polymerization curves). Detailed steps are in the Support Information.
N-GQDs were fabrication using citric acid and urea as precursors through a hydrothermal reaction [35]. Detailed steps are in the Support Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZnO nanoflowers on ITO substrates
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NFs were prepared on ITO substrates by electrochemical growth method [36]. Detailed steps are in the Support Information.

Dissolve 5 mg of N-GQDs in 10 ml of anhydrous ethanol solution to configure a 0. 5 mg/ml resuspension solution. Employing a spin coating apparatus, N-GQDs were applied to the ZnO NFs/ITO surface via five successive spins at a rotational speed of 3000 revolutions per minute for a duration of 30 s each. Subsequently, the ZnO NFs/ITO substrates adorned with the N-GQD tier were subjected to thermal treatment in a vacuum environment at a temperature of 60 °C for a period of 30 min to yield the ITO/GQDs-ZnO NFs composite structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and photoelectric measurement of UV PD devices
ce:section.ce:para: The assembled ITO/N-GQDs/ZnO NFs and PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO were positioned in a face-to-face configuration. Both junction surfaces were then affixed with high-temperature adhesive tape, while the overlapping region was uniformly coated with PVA for safeguarding, Where the valid illuminated region of the device is 0.24 cm2 (Fig. S5c–5d). Specific details are in the supporting materials.
The prepared materials were characterised morphologically and structurally using various characterisation tools. Specific details are in the supporting materials.

Fig. 1 presents a sequential assembly approach for an organic-inorganic heterojunction device, integrating ZnO NFs/ITO with PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO. The PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO and N-GQDs-ZnO NFs/ITO components were coated with PVA and assembled face-to-face to form the UV detector. The electrochemically grown ZnO was grown onto the ITO surface in a lamellar petal-like morphology (Fig. 2 a). At the same time, PEDOT-CH2OH prepared by electrochemical polymerization formed a dense surface on the ITO substrate in the form of nanofiber rods (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 1, the face-to-face assembly of the two components allows for efficient electron transfer due to the good contact between the ZnO nanoflower-like structure and the PEDOT-CH2OH nanofibrous structure. In addition, the petal-like structure not only rise the light-exposed surface region but also enhances UV light absorption, thereby boosting the UV detector's photocurrent [37].Meanwhile, the HR-TEM image (Fig. 2d) reveals the distribution of N-GQDs on the ZnO side after spin-coating, which lattice stripe spacing of ZnO is 0.27 nm, aligning with the fibrillar zincite structure's planar spacing. This verify that the ZnO nanoflowers grow along the c-axis and are single crystals with a fibrillar zincite structure [38,39].

Fig. 2d presents the EDS and elemental mapping of N-GQDs/ZnO NFs composites via TEM. The elemental distribution shows an N content of 0.6, and the mapping reveals uniform N distribution, indicating N-GQDs are well-dispersed on ZnO NFs. SEM scans (Fig. S2) of ZnO tiers before and after N-GQDs spin-coating further confirm the surface distribution of N-GQDs on ZnO. the mapping diagram in Fig. S2b, following the addition of N-GQDs, clearly shows the nitrogen (N) element, primarily attributed to N-GQDs, is now more evenly distributed across the entire ZnO layer surface. To further explore the relationships between ZnO NFs and N-GQDs, we tested both pure ZnO NFs and ZnO NFs/N-GQDs mixtures using the XPS technique, which detected zinc, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen in the composites (Fig. 2e). The Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks for ZnO were measured at 1021.56 eV and 1044.74 eV, respectively. After N-GQDs modification, these peaks transferred to 1022.27 eV and 1045.45 eV, which likely due to the interaction between ZnO and N-GQDs (Fig. 2f). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 2h shows a prominent peak at 284.8 eV (carbon-carbon bond in sp2 orbital), with additional peaks at 285.2 eV (carbon-oxygen), 286.6 eV (carbon-nitrogen), and 288.3 eV (carbon-double-bonded oxygen), verification of oxygen-containing functional groups and the successful synthesis of carbon-oxygen bonds within the composites, as referenced [40].

Fig. 2g shows the spectra of O1s and reveals distinct peaks at 531.74 eV and 532.64 eV, where the peaks corresponding to lower binding energies are O2− in the ZnO lattice, while the peaks corresponding to higher binding energies are defective O2− in ZnO [32]. From the O 1s spectra after the addition of the modifying material N-GQDs, it is found that the peaks are shifted to the lower field, and according to the fact that the lower binding energy corresponds to the lattice O2− and the higher binding energy corresponds to the defective O2−, it is found that the peaks of the defective O2− in ZnO are shifted to the lower field, and at the same time the peak intensity is enlarged, and the binding energy decreases from 532.64 to 531.53 eV after the accession of the N-GQDs. Meanwhile, the peak corresponding to ZnO lattice O2− moves from 531.74 eV to 530.33 eV, and the change of the XPS spectral peaks from O1s well corroborates the effect of doping of N-GQDs on the Vo on the ZnO surface. So as to better compare the impact of the addition of N-GQDs on the Vo concentration on the surface of ZnO, the changes in the elemental ratio content of vacancy O2− to lattice O2− before and after doping were further calculated by deconvolution of high-resolution spectra (Table S1), and the results showed that the defective O2−/lattice O2− was 54.12 %/45.88 % without addition of N-GQDs, and 75.20 %/24.80 % after the addition of N-GQDs modification. The significant effect of N-GQDs doping on the surface Vo of ZnO was strongly demonstrated by the analysis of the changes in the migration of deconvolution peaks in XPS before and after doping, and the promotion of ZnO surface oxygen vacancy generation by N-GQDs was further demonstrated by the semi-quantitative analysis of the changes in the ratio of elemental contents. However, exploring the promotion of oxygen vacancies by N-GQDs based on XPS analysis alone may be slightly thin. To further verify this, we employed the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique on both modified and unmodified ZnO samples (see Fig. S3). By analyzing the peaks at g, we can accurately determine the presence of oxygen vacancies. Materials containing oxygen vacancies usually show characteristic peaks at a g value of about 2.002 [41]. In Fig. S3, we found that the intensity of the peak corresponding to oxygen vacancies was significantly enhanced by the accession of N-GQDs. The characteristic peaks of the oxygen vacancies are observed even without the addition of the modifying materials, which further confirms the conclusion of the XPS analysis that the oxygen vacancies in pure ZnO correspond to the O2- peaks. Combining the results of the XPS and EPR analyses, it is clear that the N-GQDs have a significant promotion effect on the Vo on the ZnO surface. The N1s patterns displayed in Fig. 2i highlight clear N 1s peaks, indicating the exitence of pyridine (398.68 eV) and pyrrole (400.01 eV) [35,42], it is fully demonstrated that N atoms are successfully embedded in GQDs.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD pattern of N-GQDs/ZnO NFs, it was found that due to the low amount of composite of the modifying material N-GQDs and the low amount of product of ZnO from electrochemical growth to ITO conductive glass, the diffraction peaks of tiny ZnO were shown in the XRD pattern and the diffraction peaks of the N-GQDs did not appear, therefore, after the N-GQDs/ZnO NFs were peeled off from ITO glass, they were The analysis was carried out, and as shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, the diffraction peaks were seen to be 31.73,34.34, 36.29, 47.41, 56.45, 62.82, 66.50, 67.76, and 68.97, which corresponded to the ZnO planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201) [17], it was confirmed that the ZnO NFs have a hexagonal fibrillated zincite crystal structure. Fig. 3b shows the IR spectrum of PEDOT-CH2OH, with key peaks at 1200 cm−1 and 1041 cm−1 for C–O–C stretching, 3000 cm−1 for CH2 stretching, 3200 cm−1 for OH stretching, and 681 cm−1 for C–S–C stretching, these spectral features collectively affirm the successful electrochemical polymerization of PEDOT-CH2OH. Fig. 3c displays the IR spectra of ZnO NFs, N-GQDs/ZnO NFs. Notable peaks include OH stretching at 3300-3500 cm−1, Zn–O bending at 500-600 cm−1, and additional Zn–O bending at 1600-1700 cm−1, with a weak peak near 1700 cm−1 from ZnO molecular vibrations. After adding N-GQDs, the absorption peaks broaden, with a weak peak at 1045 cm−1 possibly from C–N vibrations and bands at 960-700 cm−1 related to N bonding, aligning with EDS results in Fig. 2d that confirm N-GQDs attachment to ZnO. From the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fig. 3d, it is obvious that ZnO exhibits powerful absorption between 300 nm and 400 nm, after incorporating N-GQDs, the UV absorption peaks shift and intensify, indicating that N-GQDs act as effective photosensitizers, enhancing optical properties by improving light responsiveness.

So as to measure the thickness of each active tier within the device, each structural tier of the device was examined using atomic force microscopy (AFM), while the thickness changes before and after the device was added to the PVA package were measured using vernier calipers, and the information of the thickness of each active tier of the specific device structure was schematically shown in Fig. S4, in which the zinc oxide was used as a n-type, PEDOT-CH2OH as a p-type, the ITO/PET conductive membrane with a thickness of 125 μm was used as the substrate electrode, which was immersed into the precursor solution for electrochemical growth, as can be seen in the device structure of Fig. 1, the conductive substrate has a part that is not immersed in the precursor solution, so the critical region between the coated and uncoated areas was selected for AFM testing to detect the thickness of the grown tier better.

Fig. 4 a shows the 2D and 3D views of the region selected for measuring the thickness of the ZnO tier, and three line regions were randomly selected for the treatment, and the thickness of the ZnO structural tier was discovered to be an average of 178 nm. Fig. 4b shows 2D and 3D views of the area selected to measure the thickness of the ZnO/N-GQDs tier and processed using the same methodology, the average thickness was found to be 856 nm and the difference between the two was calculated and the difference between the two values was found to be 678 nm for the spin-coated N-GQDs.

Fig. 4c shows the 2D and 3D views of the selected area for measuring the thickness of the conductive polymer PEDOT-CH2OH, which was also processed using the same method, and the average thickness was found to be 394 nm. The change in the thickness of the overall device before and after the addition of the PVA overlay was measured by using a vernier caliper (Fig. S5). Fig. S5a is the overall device thickness without adding PVA tier is 0.25 mm, Fig. S5b is the overall device thickness by adding the protective tier is 0.26 mm, the difference between the two is the thickness of the PVA tier, and the thickness of the protective tier PVA is found to be 5 μm after calculation. Meanwhile, in order to explore whether the addition of N-GQDs has an effect on the surface roughness of the ZnO tier, the ZnO layer before and after the addition of N-GQDs was subjected to a comparative roughness test using the AFM technique (Fig. S6), and the consequence displayed that the roughness Ra before the addition of N-GQDs was 129 nm, while the roughness Ra of the ZnO layer after the addition of N-GQDs was 86.7 nm. The larger the roughness, the larger the resulting gap, which reduces the carrier transport efficiency, increases the complex center, and limits the segregation of photogenerated carriers [43]. The addition of quantum dots fills the ZnO layer gap, reduces the roughness of the interface, and promotes the transmit of photogenerated carriers, which significantly improves the photovoltaic performance, and this phenomenon can be better detected in the subsequent comparison of the performance as well.

The ZnO/ZnO-based UV photodetector (Fig. 5 a) exhibits a typical response waveform but achieves only reaches about 8 nA photocurrent within 10 s of UV exposure, failing to reach equilibrium, alongside prolonged response time (Fig. 5b). In contrast, the PEDOT/ZnO device (Fig. 5c) demonstrates a stable photocurrent of 40 nA with a well-defined waveform. The initial UV irradiation triggers an abrupt current spike (Ipyro + photo) due to ZnO's pyroelectric effect, transitioning to a stable Iphoto + thermo under continuous irradiation, followed by rapid decay to dark current upon UV removal. PEDOT integration reduces response times to 0.060/0.050 s (rise/fall, Fig. 5d), attributed to its dual role as a hole transport tier and a p-n heterojunction enhancer with ZnO. Further optimization is observed in the PEDOT-CH2OH/ZnO device (Fig. 5e), where the I-t curve stabilizes at 66 nA after 10 s UV exposure. Hydroxymethylation introduces surface hydroxyl groups that electrochemically stabilize ZnO Vo, improving carrier transport. Concurrently, polar OH groups generate thermally responsive dipoles, amplifying pyroelectric coupling effects. In addition, the ZnO's Vo are filled in during the electrochemical growth flow, which create the absence of Vo which affects the carrier transport, while after hydroxymethylation, the two materials are composite, the hydroxyl functional group on the polymer surface can stabilization of Vo on the surface of ZnO, which improves its ability to capture and bind electrons while facilitating the electron transport ability, and at the same time, after hydroxymethylation, due to the polarity of the OH functional group, more dipoles are introduced into the material, and when the material is subjected to heat, these dipoles would tend to rearrange in the direction of the temperature gradient enhances the pyroelectric effect [44], which in turn also promotes the reverse pyroelectric effect. The reverse pyroelectric spike can also be clearly seen in Fig. 5f. As shown in Fig. 5g for the device composed of ZnO modified by N-GQDs, the device still exhibits the typical stable waveform behavior, and the device shows a stronger photoelectric effect with higher spikes and photocurrents up to about 100 nA compared to ZnO/PEDOT-CH2OH alone, in which the existence of N-GQDs improves the UV absorption of the device, which adds the photoelectric response, the photocurrent increases at the same time.The response times to 0.078/0.093 s (rise/fall, Fig. 5h), which does not increase significantly, and the performance of the photoresponse rate is fast, and the optical intensity is constantly increasing after the addition of the PEDOT class of conductive polymers as a hole transporting tier, at the same time, the photoresponse time does not change significantly, and the time is short, and the response rate is fast, which is better. The excellent photoelectric properties of PEDOT-like conductive polymers were verified. The photovoltaic performance of ZnO NFs/PEDOT-CH2OH devices with and without N-GQDs was tested under 365 nm UV light at 0 V. Fig. 5i shows the photocurrent rises with light intensities (0.32–2.40 mW/cm2). Fig. 5j highlights a notable photocurrent increase after N-GQD modification. Due to the addition of N elements, the generation of Vo is essentially stimulated, while the existence of a lots of oxygen-containing functional groups like carboxyl groups on the QDs as well as hydroxyl functional groups in the polymer after hydroxymethylation further stabilizes the Vo, thus affecting the photovoltaic effect of the device. However, high power densities cause photocarrier shielding in ZnO NFs and saturated photovoltaic effects [45], so the photovoltaic thermoelectric effect doesn't change much. The pyroelectric-induced peak current is less pronounced than in devices without N-GQDs.

Where e is a primary charge.

The ITO/ZnO NFs/PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO device's PR and D∗ values, calculated via the formulae, were found to decline as the power density rose, with peak values achieved at 0.32 mW/cm2 (Fig. S7). This indicates strong low-power UV detection capability. Post N-GQD modification, PR and D∗ values rose, underscoring enhanced photoresponsiveness and confirming the device's high photosensitivity. As per Eq. (2), D∗ is direct proportion to PR, a relationship borne out by Fig. S7a and S7b.

Another key UV PD detection parameter is the device's rise/fall time, defined as the time taken for the current to go from 10 % to 90 % of the biggest photocurrent (rise time) and to drop from 90 % to 10 % (fall time) [47]. From Fig. S8a–S8d, the device's response time (0.078s/0.093s) at low light intensity (0.56 mW/cm2), indicating fast electron-hole separation and carrier migration. But as light intensity rises, the response time nearly doubles compared to the initial 0.56 mW/cm2 intensity and reaches twice the original photocurrent's response time. Yet, further light intensity increases cause little or no response time change, or even a slight decrease. This is because UV light excites N-GQDs' electrons, producing many photogenerated carriers. The quantum effect of N-GQDs enhances electron and carrier separation under UV light, thus extending the response time. So, increasing power density within a certain range may decrease the device's response time, but at high power density, the response time lengthens because the surge in photogenerated carriers impedes effective carrier separation in p-n heterojunctions.

To better verify the device's rectification characteristics, prove its photovoltaic mechanism, and provide strong evidence for p-n heterojunction formation, the I–V characteristics of the assembled devices were studied. Fig. 6 a and b shows the I–V curves of devices with and without N-GQD modification, while Fig. 6c and d presents their semi-log I–V curves. From the forward bias voltage (0–0.2 V) I–V curves (Fig. 6a and b), it is evident that under 365 nm UV irradiation, the output current increases with power density, with no current decrease trend. This is mainly due to the higher light intensity increasing the electron-hole pair generation rate. Comparing the output current before and after N-GQD addition, a significant current increase is observed at the same optical power density, indicating that N-GQDs enhance ZnO surface Vo formation, thereby improving carrier generation and separation [48]. The current generation at 0 V, shown in Fig. 6a and b, confirms the UV photodetectors' self-powered capability. From the semi-log I–V curves in Fig. 6c and d, the nonlinear and asymmetric curves indicate good rectification and typical p-n junction behavior, further confirming the photovoltaic mechanism [49,50].

In order to study the dominant current mechanism under the built-in electric field constructed after the device forms a p-n heterojunction, the ideal diode factor n is calculated based on the semilogarithmic current-voltage curves, and the dominant current mechanism is judged based on the magnitude of the value of n, which shows that the diffusion current is dominant when n = 1, and is dominated by the composite current when n = 2. A wide range of current dominant mechanisms exists when n is between 1 and 2, and when n is larger than 2, the leakage current dominates [51]. The rectification characteristics can be found from the semi-logarithmic current-voltage curves (Fig. 6c and d), while the p-n heterojunction current-voltage relationship is usually depicted as a function related to the voltage (V) [52]. The calculations were carried out under the voltage of 0–0.2 V was selected, and according to the formula calculations (Eq. S(3)), it was found that the n value of the device (Table S2) was calculated at different power densities (0.32–2.40 mW/cm2) before the addition of the N-GQDs when the bias voltage was small, and it was found that all the n values were larger than 2 and closer to 2, which proved that the device dominated the composite currents at this point in time, and it was found to be more than 2 at the time of the addition of the N-GQDs modification, also calculated under different power density (0.32–2.40 mW/cm2) conditions, it is found that the n-value of the device ranges from 1 to 2 and is close to the ideal diode factor of 1. From the comparative change in the n-value of the two, it is found that the device converges to the ideal diode behavior after the addition of the modified material, further proving that diffusion current increases in the few carriers on both laterals of the heterojunction [50]. While the devices were tested with the voltage in the range of (-1-1V), the forward bias voltage (0–1V) range was selected for calculation, and it was found that the n values ranged from 8 to 9 before the addition of the modifying material, and after the addition of the modifying material, the n values were in the range of 6–7, it is found that the value of n is much larger than 1 at higher forward bias, at which the tunneling current acts as key role in the ZnO/PEDOT-CH2OH heterojunction [53]. Since the barrier height formed at the heterojunction screen act as a role in the photocurrent flow at the interface, under UV illumination, the generated photocarriers should be transferred from one side of the interface to the other by overcoming the built-in barrier and depletion region, so the influence of the built-in barrier on the overall device must be considered, and the value of the saturation current I0 at the extrapolated 0 bias voltage is derived from Fig. 6c and d. In this way, the built-in barrier can be calculated from Eq. S(2). ϕb, after calculation, it is found that the built-in potential barrier remains around 0.68 eV before and after the accession of the modifying material N-GQDs, and it is also proved that the built-in barrier height is similar under the darkness and different light conditions, remaining between 0.68 and 0.70 eV, and the lower barrier height is helpful for the promotion of carrier migration, which establish a stable foundation for the realization of a good rectification effect.

For evaluating the flexible UV detector's performance under bending, it was attached to disposable mask straps. Fig. 7 a–f displays the device's physical views at various bends, tested under these conditions. Fig. 7g and h shows the I-t curves at 0.32 mW/cm2 light (365 nm UV) for bending tests, showing Ipyro + photo at 110.36 nA at 30° and 115.12 nA at 60°. As the bend angle increases, Ipyro + photo decreases, maintaining a reproducible waveform at each angle, indicating stable performance and flexibility. Bending slightly improves performance by increasing organic-inorganic junction contact area. Enhanced pyroelectric effect after bending creates sharp photocurrent peaks. N-GQDs embed better in ZnO, maintaining detection performance, flexibility, and stability, especially at small bending angles. Close ZnO NF contact boosts photoelectric effect, and organic-inorganic junction contact is improved, stabilizing electron orbitals under bending. However, increased bending angle reduces light exposure area, decreasing photo-generated carriers from photovoltaic and piezoelectric effects. Bending tests result in ZnO NF cracking (Fig. S9a–b), impeding electron transfer and photocurrent. N-GQD integration bridges cracks, supporting carrier transport and improving electron transport. ZnO's high piezoelectric constant lets it modulate energy bands at the junction with piezoelectric polarization from external strains, controlling carrier formation, separation, transport, and recombination to mitigate ZnO film tearing during bending. The organic phase as nanofibers and ZnO nanoflowers enhance bending contact, stabilizing electron transmission orbitals.

As shown in Fig. 7h, the device underwent 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 bending cycles at 0.32 mW/cm2. Post-bending, I-t curves were tested in the straightened state. Results indicate relatively stable performance with minor fluctuations. Sharp spikes, more pronounced than in the unbent state, appeared before 600 bends but with significant photocurrent decline. After 800 bends, the spike vanished. By 1000 bends, the device maintained a stable photocurrent but lacked an initial UV irradiation peak, which due to the degradation of ZnO and N-GQDs structure after extensive bending, causing temperature sensitivity loss. Under large-angle bending, ZnO on the flexible substrate scatters carriers, increasing their transport path. Fractures from bending cause electric field concentration, accelerating carrier recombination. Additionally, higher electric fields lead to overheating, accelerating material aging and performance reduction. Nevertheless, the N-GQDs: ZnO/PEDOT-CH2OH device maintains better UV detection vitality even after many bending cycles. Similarly, so as to verify the reproducibility of the devices, 10 devices were prepared under identical conditions. The photocurrents from two switching cycles were selected and plotted as normal distribution graphs (Fig. S10–11). Fig. S10 shows the photocurrents of devices without N-GQDs modification, while Fig. S11 shows those with N-GQDs under varying light intensities. The results indicate that the photocurrents of different devices remain constant under the same light intensity, with the overall error within 20 nA. In summary, the flexibility and stability tests demonstrate that the N-GQDs: ZnO/PEDOT-CH2OH device has good reproducibility and maintains UV detection activity even after extensive bending.

Comparing the devices developed in this work with other flexible UV detectors (Table 1 ), the devices in this work stand out for not needing applied bias or high light intensity to achieve high performance. For instance, Peng [54] et al. created a flexible UV PD based on GaN/ZnO heterojunction that operates at 325 nm with 38.4 W/cm2 light intensity, a level over 10 times greater than our device. Zhang [58] et al.'s Ag NW@ZnO NRs-based flexible UV detector had a responsivity of 0.0065 mA/W at 5V and 1.3 mW/cm2 (365 nm) light intensity. Lin [59] et al.'s ZnS/SnO2 heterostructure-based flexible UV detector achieved a photocurrent of 47.5 pA at 10 V, 17.88 mW/cm2 (380 nm) light intensity, with a rise/fall time of 4.2 s/1.5 s.

In contrast, the present device selects the safer and more applicable UV light of 365 nm as the light source. Compared with other devices shown in the comparison table, it can realize sensitive and fast high-performance detection under the conditions of long wavelength, low light intensity and no external bias voltage. It performed well at 0 V, 0.32 mW/cm2 power density (365 nm), and room temperature, with a biggest responsivity of 1.31 mA/W and a biggest detectivity of 4.0346 × 1010 Jones. Based on the optimal rise/fall time it possesses (0.078s/0.093s), it is judged that this device has a fast carrier migration rate and photogenerated electron-hole separation rate. The detection capability is maintained even after bending up to 150°, and the device can remain active after up to 1000 bending cycles. Meanwhile, from the comparison table, it is easy to see that the ITO/N-GQDs/ZnO NFs/PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO device has a fast detection capability for low-power-density light irradiation, and the addition of N-GQDs also greatly improves the responsivity and the detection rate under low-power-density light irradiation.

In heterojunction structures, the energy bands of two materials are docked according to their energy level differences. In order to better understand the electron-hole segregation and migration inside the device under light and dark conditions, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and mapping of its mechanism were carried out, which yielded a LUMO of −0.98 eV and a HOMO of −4.35 eV for PEDOT-CH2OH (Fig. S10b–S10c). Meanwhile, ZnO, as a semiconducting metal oxide, has a valence band of −7.8 eV and a conduction band of −4.5 eV, and this horizontal shift of the energy bands is more helpful for the separation of photogenerated carriers [61,62]. This heterojunction is a gap-breaking heterojunction in terms of the difference in energy band docking between the two materials [63], and for the ZnO NFs/N-GQDs/PEDOT-CH2OH heterojunction, the built-in electric field generated in the depletion region enables the photogenerated electron-hole pairs to split faster. The hole moves to the p-type PEDOT-CH2OH, while more electrons migrate to the conduction band of ZnO driven by the electric field to further promote the separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes, and the device is able to separate the carriers effectively in this way, and isolate the electrons and holes in the conduction bands and valence bands of the different materials to form an effective separation region, which enhances the efficiency of the photovoltaic effect.

To better illustrate the photogenerated carrier transport mechanism of the devices under UV irradiation, schematics of photogenerated electron-hole transport and energy bands for different devices under UV irradiation were plotted (Fig. 8 a and b). The left side of Fig. 8a shows that the photovoltaic performance of UV detector constructed from ZnO/ZnO is affected due to the numerous composite centers in the ZnO lattice [22]. When forming a p-n heterojunction with PEDOT-CH2OH, the energy band diagram on the right side of Fig. 8a indicates that this process relies on the horizontal migration of the energy bands of the polymer and ZnO, and the construction of a heterojunction that breaks the bandgap. This brings about the separation of electrons and holes, with electrons migrating to the ZnO layer and holes moving to the polymer tier, thereby improving the effective split of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the energy band diagram on the right side of Fig. 8b shows that when N-GQDs are loaded onto the ZnO surface, they can enhance the Vo effect on the ZnO surface, improving the binding ability of photogenerated electrons. The conjugation and hydrogen bonding between N-GQDs and the polymer tier enable the two materials to generate more photogenerated electron-hole pairs under UV irradiation, thereby reinforce the photocurrent. The photogenerated electron-hole transport schematic on the left side of Fig. 8b well depicts these phenomena.

To better understand the energy of the electrons inside the material and the electron injection efficiency between the material and the external circuit, i.e. the minimum energy required for the electrons to move from the inside of the solid to the vacuum energy level. If the work function of the material is high, it means that the electrons do not escape easily [64]. Also, during contact or electron transfer between different materials, electrons from a material with a low work function will be transferred to a material with a high work function. This is because electrons always tend to move from the low figure of merit material (low energy state) to the high figure of merit material (high energy state) to reach thermodynamic equilibrium [65].

As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, the DFT calculations of the materials reveal that the figure of merit of N-GQDs is 3.646 eV and that of ZnO NFs is 8.425 eV, in which the N-GQDs have a better advantage of a lower Fermi energy level compared to the ZnO NFs, which also suggests that the electrons from the N-GQDs are more prone to escape. The higher figure of merit of the ZnO NFs means that it is more inclined to inject electrons into the internal material than N-GQDs, which acts as an electron acceptor in the heterojunction, and this electronmigration can effectively separate the photogenerated electrons and holes and enhance the photovoltaic conversion efficiency.The binding energy is the energy difference resulting from the interaction of two materials in a heterojunction, which usually affects their energy band docking and determines the efficiency of carrier (electron and hole) separation, thus reflecting the strength of the interaction between the materials. Therefore, the ZnO NFs and N-GQDs as well as PEDOT-CH2OH and ZnO NFs/N-GQDs heterojunction interfaces were investigated using DFT calculations. As shown in Fig. 9c and d, it can be found that the binding energy of N-GQDs-ZnO NFs is −12.32 eV and that of N-GQDs/ZnO/PEDOT-CH2OH is −1.04 eV, which indicates that the interaction between N-GQDs and ZnO NFs is stronger and the contact is tight, which contributes to the effective transfer of electrons between the two and promotes the carrier separation, thus improving the responsivity of the UV detector. It is found that the binding energy changes significantly and is less than 0 eV after the composite conductive polymer, which further proves that a heterojunction is formed at the interface between the two, which contributes to the generation, separation, and transmission of photogenerated carriers, and ultimately improves the performance of flexible self-powered UV photodetectors [66,67].

In summary, in this paper, a flexible UV photodetector with the structure of PET/ITO/N-GQDs/ZnO-NFs/PEDOT-CH2OH/ITO/PET has been developed by skillfully utilizing the photovoltaic materials of PEDOT-CH2OH and ZnO NFs modified by N-CQDs, and the detector exhibits excellent flexibility and stability as well as excellent responsivity and low response time. The detector has excellent flexibility and stability, as well as excellent responsiveness and low response time. The Vo's electron-hole binding capability in the ZnO material itself, along with the combined photoelectric, thermoelectric, and piezoelectric properties, are what give the device its remarkable UV detecting capabilities. The combination of quantum dots reduces the surface roughness of ZnO from 129 nm to 86.7 nm, which in turn minimizes the carrier complexation centers and also improves the electron-hole separation efficiency, increases the photoelectric current, and optimizes the carrier transport path. Meanwhile, the p-n heterojunction formed between ZnO and PEDOT-CH2OH is utilized to realize self-powering, and the oxygen vacancy defects are stabilized by promoting the generation of oxygen vacancies through N-GQDs, as well as the formation of Vo+-OH bonding between oxygen-rich functional groups (OH) at its edges and the surface oxygen vacancies of ZnO NFs. Meanwhile, using a special face-to-face assembly method, the nanofiber-like conducting polymer PEDOT-CH2 OH can be fully wound and filled into the ZnO NFs, which improves the contact area of the organic-inorganic heterojunction, and, in addition, reduces the roughness of the surface of the inorganic semiconductor by embedding the quantum dots, which ensures the close contact of the organic-inorganic photosensitive tier, and is able to provide an effective carrier transport path for the support architecture, which helps to maintain the relative structural integrity during the bending process. The device has a peak responsivity of 1.31 mA/W, a peak detectivity of 4.03 × 1010 Jones, and an optimal rise/fall time of 0.078s/0.093 s when exposed to 365 nm UV light with a bias voltage of 0 V and room temperature. The responsivity is ten times higher than that of the ZnO/ZnO UV detector, and the device still exhibits detecting activity even after 1000 bends at 150° when the light response is maintained. This work offers a workable approach to investigating how oxygen vacancies improve the performance of ZnO-based UV photodetectors and creating flexible, high-performance, and quick-response ZnO-based UV detectors.
